---
title: "Climate finance scholarship crystallized at Copenhagen, not Paris"
author:
  - name: Minh Ha-Duong
    affiliations:
      - name: "CNRS, CIRED"
        city: "Nogent-sur-Marne"
        country: "France"
    email: "minh.ha-duong@cnrs.fr"
    corresponding: true
date: "2026-04-13"
metadata-files: [ncc-analysis-vars.yml]
# NCC-specific variables (until compute_vars.py gains an ncc-analysis entry,
# these are also embedded here as fallback; the vars file overrides if present)
corpus_total: "31,713"
corpus_total_approx: "32,000"
corpus_core: "2,648"
corpus_core_threshold: "50"
corpus_sources: "6"
analysis_corpus_n: "28,015"
emb_dimensions: "1024"
bim_dbic_embedding: "1,255"
bim_dbic_tfidf: "15,345"
bim_corr: "0.77"
bim_dbic_2007_2014: "-44"
bim_dbic_post2015: "951"
bim_dbic_pre2007: "-20"
bim_var_pct: "2.1"
bim_core_dbic_embedding: "371"
pca_emb_pc1_var_pct: "3.9"
pca_emb_pc2_var_pct: "3.5"
break_copenhagen_z: "2.15"
break_2007_z: "2.59"
break_2013_z: "1.59"
bibliography: bibliography/main.bib
format:
  pdf:
    pdf-engine: xelatex
    number-sections: false
    cite-method: citeproc
    include-in-header:
      - text: |
          \usepackage{fancyhdr}
          \fancypagestyle{plain}{
            \fancyhf{}
            \fancyhead[C]{\small\itshape For submission to Nature Climate Change}
            \fancyfoot[C]{\thepage}
            \renewcommand{\headrulewidth}{0pt}
          }
---

<!-- NCC Analysis format: up to 3,000 words main text, 150-word abstract,
     up to 6 display items, ~50 references, Online Methods up to ~3,000 words.
     Title: 90 characters max (current: 65 characters).
     References use numbered Nature style. -->

<!-- Phase 2→3 contract: this NCC analysis consumes
     - figures/fig_ncc_divergence.png   (@fig-ncc-divergence)
     - figures/fig_ncc_core.png         (@fig-ncc-core)
     - figures/fig_ncc_bimodality.png   (@fig-ncc-bimodality)
     - figures/fig_ncc_alluvial.png     (@fig-ncc-alluvial)
     Any change to these outputs requires rebuilding the document. -->

**Abstract.**
The conceptual categories through which climate finance is governed have not changed in fifteen years. Analysing {{< meta corpus_total_approx >}} works with sentence-transformer embeddings, we detect a structural break in climate finance scholarship at 2009, coinciding with the Copenhagen $100 billion pledge, but none at 2015 (Paris Agreement) or later. The most-cited papers show no structural break at all: the field's intellectual core has been stable since its formation. A secondary but persistent axis of polarization separates efficiency-oriented and accountability-oriented scholarship; this divide sharpened after 2015 but operates within the same categorical framework established at Copenhagen. These findings suggest that current governance debates---from the New Collective Quantified Goal to Article 6 implementation---are fought using a conceptual toolkit that predates the Paris Agreement by half a decade. If the accounting categories are locked in, reforming climate finance governance requires changing the categories themselves.

<!-- MAIN TEXT (~3,000 words) -->

The Paris Agreement is widely assumed to have transformed climate finance. In 2015, 196 parties committed to making finance flows consistent with low-carbon pathways (Article 2.1c), replacing the Kyoto Protocol's rigid annexes with nationally determined contributions. Yet when we examine what scholars actually write about climate finance---not what negotiators agreed, but what the research community produces---the transformation is invisible. The conceptual categories of climate finance scholarship crystallized around the 2009 Copenhagen Accord and its $100 billion pledge. They have not changed since.

This finding matters because scholarly categories are not passive descriptions. The accounting frameworks through which climate finance is measured (Rio markers, mobilised private finance methodologies, concessionality thresholds) were constructed by economists working in international organisations, particularly the OECD Development Assistance Committee [@corfee-morlot2009; @stadelmann2011]. Once institutionalized, these categories generated increasing returns: training programmes, reporting systems, databases, and careers organized around them [@pierson2000]. The result is a form of epistemic lock-in analogous to the carbon lock-in that Unruh identified in energy systems [@unruh2000; @seto2016]---here applied not to physical infrastructure but to the conceptual apparatus through which governance operates.

We analyse a corpus of {{< meta corpus_total >}} works on climate finance (1990--2024) drawn from {{< meta corpus_sources >}} bibliographic sources (OpenAlex, Semantic Scholar, ISTEX, bibCNRS, SciSpace, and curated grey literature). Using multilingual sentence-transformer embeddings (BAAI/bge-m3, {{< meta emb_dimensions >}} dimensions), we test three questions: when did the field's semantic structure change most sharply? Does the most-cited literature show the same pattern? And is the efficiency-accountability divide documented qualitatively [@care_weber2023; @roberts2021] visible as a quantitative structure in the embedding space?

## Structural breaks converge on Copenhagen

We detect structural breaks using sliding-window divergence on the embedding space, combining two complementary metrics: Jensen-Shannon divergence on cluster-proportion vectors (sensitive to thematic redistribution) and cosine distance on embedding centroids (sensitive to semantic drift). At baseline, the analysis identifies candidate breakpoints at 2007 (cosine, z = {{< meta break_2007_z >}}) and 2013 (JS, z = {{< meta break_2013_z >}}). No break appears near 2015, 2016, or any later year. The COP milestones that dominate policy narratives produce no corresponding discontinuity in the scholarly literature's structure.

To test whether these signals reflect genuine discontinuities or gradual transitions, we apply a censored-gap variant that removes transitional years from the comparison windows. When two years are censored on each side of the candidate boundary (k=2), a single breakpoint survives: **2009** (cosine, z = {{< meta break_copenhagen_z >}}). The 2007 signal migrates within tolerance; the 2013 signal vanishes, indicating a gradual redistribution rather than a sharp break. The convergence on 2009 aligns with what historians of climate economics identify as the crystallization moment: the Copenhagen Accord's $100 billion pledge transformed climate finance from a diffuse set of concerns into a consolidated field with shared categories, reporting standards, and institutional infrastructure.

Volume-confound checks confirm that these breakpoints are not artefacts of corpus growth. All correlations between publication volume and divergence scores remain below |r| = 0.5 (@fig-ncc-divergence).

## The core literature shows no break at all

The most informative contrast emerges from comparing the full corpus with its most-cited subset. Restricting the analysis to {{< meta corpus_core >}} papers cited {{< meta corpus_core_threshold >}} or more times---the field's intellectual backbone---eliminates all structural breaks in the 2005--2020 range. The only signal is a boundary artefact at 2023, where recent papers have not yet accumulated sufficient citations to enter the core (@fig-ncc-core).

This absence is the finding, not a null result. The 2009 break in the full corpus was driven by the influx of new scholarship working *within* categories that the core had already established. The influential papers---those that set the field's conceptual parameters---show no thematic discontinuity across the entire observation window. What changed after Copenhagen was the volume and disciplinary breadth of climate finance research, not the conceptual structure. Paris, Glasgow, and Baku each generated surges of new publications, but these publications operated within the same categorical architecture: mitigation versus adaptation, public versus private, bilateral versus multilateral, additional versus non-additional.

This pattern resembles what Kuhn called normal science [@kuhn1962]: puzzle-solving within a stable paradigm. The categories set during crystallization define the problems that climate finance scholars solve. But the analogy has a sting. Normal science in physics or chemistry involves categories refined through experimental testing. The categories of climate finance were designed for diplomatic accounting---they accommodate ambiguity by construction [@weikmans_roberts2019]. "What counts as climate finance?" remains unanswered after three decades because the categories were built to keep the question open, and their stability reflects institutional convenience rather than intellectual adequacy.

## The efficiency-accountability divide is real but secondary

Qualitative accounts have long noted a tension between two orientations in climate finance scholarship: one emphasizing economic efficiency, private capital mobilization, and market mechanisms; the other emphasizing accountability, public obligation, and the additionality of reported flows [@care_weber2023; @roberts2021; @skovgaard2017]. We test whether this divide is visible as a quantitative structure.

Projecting the corpus onto a supervised seed axis (cosine similarity to efficiency-associated versus accountability-associated terms), a two-component Gaussian mixture model is strongly preferred over a single component ($\Delta$BIC = {{< meta bim_dbic_embedding >}} on embeddings; {{< meta bim_dbic_tfidf >}} on independent TF-IDF validation; cross-method correlation r = {{< meta bim_corr >}}). The field distributes into two distinct concentrations, not one.

The temporal structure of this bimodality tells a specific story. During the crystallization period (2007--2014), the distribution is unimodal ($\Delta$BIC = {{< meta bim_dbic_2007_2014 >}}): efficiency-oriented and accountability-oriented work coexisted without sharp separation. The divide emerges after 2015 ($\Delta$BIC = {{< meta bim_dbic_post2015 >}}), when the two orientations crystallized into distinct communities with limited overlap. The Paris Agreement did not restructure the field's conceptual space, but it sharpened the division within it. As the $100 billion accounting disputes intensified [@roberts2021; @toetzke2022], scholars sorted into opposing camps defined by their stance on measurement---not by the topics they studied (@fig-ncc-bimodality).

However, this divide is not the field's primary axis of variation. Principal component analysis places the efficiency-accountability axis on PC2 ({{< meta pca_emb_pc2_var_pct >}}% of variance), not PC1 ({{< meta pca_emb_pc1_var_pct >}}%, which separates adaptation research from CDM engineering). The divide is genuine, cross-validated, and persistent---but it is secondary to the field's topical structure. Unsupervised analysis alone would not have identified it as a coherent dimension.

## Stable thematic composition

An alluvial diagram of cluster membership across three data-derived periods (pre-2007, 2007--2014, post-2015) confirms visual stability in the field's thematic architecture. The same six clusters---identifiable through their top TF-IDF terms as carbon markets, green bonds/ESG, adaptation/vulnerability, energy transitions, development finance, and climate policy/governance---persist across all three periods. Their relative proportions shift modestly: carbon markets contract after 2012 while green finance expands. But the categorical structure itself remains fixed (@fig-ncc-alluvial).

This compositional stability corroborates the break-detection findings from an independent angle. The field grew by roughly an order of magnitude between 2007 and 2024, absorbing contributions from environmental science, development studies, financial economics, and political science. Yet this growth occurred within channels cut during crystallization, not through the formation of new thematic territories.

## Categories frozen, governance challenged

If the conceptual apparatus of climate finance scholarship has not changed since Copenhagen, what follows for governance? The $300 billion New Collective Quantified Goal agreed at Baku in 2024 is being operationalized using reporting frameworks inherited from the $100 billion era: the same Rio markers, the same mobilised-private-finance attribution methods, the same disputes over concessionality [@stadelmann2011; @weikmans_roberts2019]. Even the formal negotiation process bears the mark of this stability: Article 6 of the Paris Agreement, which replaced the Kyoto Protocol's CDM, was negotiated for a decade using the same conceptual toolkit---additionality, baseline-and-credit, corresponding adjustments---that the CDM had introduced, and the first credits under the new mechanism have yet to be issued.

The objection that stable categories signal a healthy, mature field deserves direct engagement. In experimental sciences, shared categories are refined through empirical testing. But climate finance's accounting categories were designed for diplomatic flexibility, not precision. When the governance challenge evolves---loss and damage emerges as a formal UNFCCC category, Article 2.1c demands systemic financial-sector transformation, the NCQG triples the headline number---while the accounting apparatus stays fixed, the result is not stability but stagnation. New policy questions are forced into old categorical boxes. Loss-and-damage scholarship, rather than generating new analytical frameworks, has been absorbed into existing clusters. Climate justice critiques, rather than restructuring the field, have sharpened the accountability pole of a pre-existing divide [@gifford_sauls2024].

The carbon lock-in analogy is direct [@unruh2000]. Fossil fuel infrastructure persists because technological and institutional co-evolution generates increasing returns to the existing system, even when alternatives exist. The accounting categories of climate finance persist for the same reason: reporting systems, institutional mandates, training programmes, and scholarly careers have co-evolved around them, regardless of their analytical adequacy. Path dependence in knowledge production, like path dependence in energy systems, requires deliberate intervention to break [@pierson2000].

What would breaking category lock-in look like? Not a new accounting methodology---the field already has more of those than it can reconcile---but a reconceptualization of what climate finance is *for*. The shift from "how much money is flowing?" to "are financial systems consistent with climate goals?" (Article 2.1c) points in this direction, but our data shows that scholarship has not yet followed. The field remains organized around flow-counting categories that predate this reframing by a decade.

Three limitations bound these findings. First, we use a single embedding model (BAAI/bge-m3); domain-specific alternatives might produce different cluster structures. Second, the citation-based core subset is biased toward older papers, which mechanically suppresses recent structural change. Third, the framework detects *semantic* stability, which may coexist with institutional or political changes invisible to text analysis. Full methodological details, robustness checks, and replication code are provided in a companion paper [Ha-Duong, 2026, companion preprint].

\newpage

## Online Methods

### Corpus construction

The corpus comprises {{< meta corpus_total >}} works on climate finance assembled from {{< meta corpus_sources >}} bibliographic sources: OpenAlex (primary, via premium API), Semantic Scholar (supplementary metadata and citations), ISTEX (French-language coverage), bibCNRS (CNRS institutional bibliography), SciSpace (teaching materials), and a curated grey-literature collection (OECD, World Bank, UNFCCC, IMF working papers and reports). The assembly pipeline performs four sequential steps: source merging and deduplication on normalised DOIs and fuzzy title matching; metadata enrichment (abstracts, citation counts, venue information); quality flagging using both rule-based filters and LLM-assisted relevance scoring; and a final policy-based filter that retains works published 1990--2024 with confirmed relevance to climate finance. The resulting corpus spans five languages (English {{< meta corpus_total >}} works; Portuguese, Spanish, French, Chinese, Japanese, and German in smaller proportions). A core subset of {{< meta corpus_core >}} papers with cited_by_count $\geq$ {{< meta corpus_core_threshold >}} represents the field's most influential works. Full construction details, audit trail, and validation statistics are documented in the companion data descriptor.

### Sentence-transformer embeddings

All works with available titles are encoded using BAAI/bge-m3, a multilingual sentence-transformer producing {{< meta emb_dimensions >}}-dimensional dense vectors. This model was chosen for its multilingual capability (placing English, French, Chinese, and other languages into a shared semantic space without translation) and its performance on retrieval benchmarks. Embeddings are computed from title text only, as abstracts are unavailable for approximately 30% of the corpus. Of the {{< meta corpus_total >}} refined works, {{< meta analysis_corpus_n >}} have both embeddings and publication years in the 1990--2024 range, forming the analysis corpus.

### Sliding-window divergence

We detect structural breaks by comparing the semantic composition of consecutive time windows. For each candidate boundary year $t$, we define a "before" window ($[t-w, t-1]$) and an "after" window ($[t+1, t+w]$), where $w$ is the half-width (tested at $w = 2, 3, 4$). Two metrics quantify the divergence:

1. **Jensen-Shannon divergence (JS):** Documents are assigned to $k=6$ clusters via K-means on embeddings. The cluster-proportion vectors of the before and after windows are compared using JS divergence. This metric is sensitive to thematic redistribution---a shift in which topics are represented---even if the overall semantic centre remains stable.

2. **Cosine distance:** The centroid (mean embedding vector) of each window is computed and compared via cosine distance. This metric is sensitive to semantic drift---a shift in *what* the literature is about at an aggregate level---even if the relative proportions of topics remain stable.

For each metric and window size, the divergence time series is z-score normalised. A candidate breakpoint is identified as a local maximum with z > 1.5, robust across at least two of the three window sizes.

### Censored-gap variant

The baseline comparison places before and after windows immediately adjacent to the boundary year. To test whether detected breaks survive the removal of transitional years, we introduce a censored gap of $k$ years on each side: the before window becomes $[t-w-k, t-1-k]$ and the after window becomes $[t+1+k, t+w+k]$. We report results for $k = 0$ (baseline), $k = 1$, and $k = 2$. A break that persists at $k = 2$---with four years of data removed around the candidate boundary---is unlikely to reflect a gradual transition.

### Volume-confound check

To rule out the possibility that divergence peaks are artefacts of corpus growth (more papers in later periods mechanically increasing between-window differences), we compute the Pearson correlation between the divergence time series and the publication-count time series for each metric and window size. All correlations remain below |r| = 0.5.

### Bimodality testing

We test for polarization along the efficiency-accountability axis using a supervised seed-axis approach. Two lists of seed terms are defined: efficiency-associated ("efficiency," "leverage," "mobilisation," "private capital," "de-risking," "blended finance," "market mechanisms") and accountability-associated ("accountability," "additionality," "transparency," "equity," "obligation," "public finance," "loss and damage"). Each document's embedding is projected onto the axis connecting the centroids of these two seed-term sets, producing a scalar score.

We fit one-component and two-component Gaussian mixture models to the resulting score distribution and compare them using the Bayesian Information Criterion (BIC). A positive $\Delta$BIC (= BIC$_1$ $-$ BIC$_2$) favours the two-component model, indicating bimodality. As an independent validation, we repeat the analysis using TF-IDF representations instead of embeddings.

Temporal decomposition splits the corpus into three periods (pre-2007, 2007--2014, post-2015) and tests for bimodality within each, tracking the emergence and sharpening of the divide.

### PCA and axis detection

To determine whether the efficiency-accountability divide is the field's primary or secondary axis of variation, we compute principal components on the {{< meta emb_dimensions >}}-dimensional embedding space. The cosine correlation between each PC and the supervised seed axis indicates which unsupervised components recover the divide. We report the variance explained by each PC and the $\Delta$BIC of Gaussian mixture models fitted to each PC's score distribution.

### Data and code availability

The corpus assembly pipeline, embedding generation, break detection, bimodality testing, and all figure-generation scripts are available at [repository URL]. The analysis uses only titles and publication metadata; no full-text access is required for replication. Raw bibliographic data from OpenAlex and Semantic Scholar are available through their respective APIs; the ISTEX and bibCNRS subsets are available through French institutional access. The curated grey-literature collection is included in the replication archive.

### AI disclosure

This manuscript was drafted with assistance from Claude (Anthropic), used as a writing and editing tool. All analytical decisions, interpretations, and arguments are the author's. The computational pipeline uses no LLM components in the analysis itself (embeddings are from a dedicated sentence-transformer model, not a generative LLM).

## Acknowledgements

This work was supported by CNRS (Section 41). The author thanks the OpenAlex team for API access.

## Author contributions

M.H.-D. designed the study, built the corpus and analysis pipeline, and wrote the manuscript.

## Competing interests

The author declares no competing interests.

## References {.unnumbered}

::: {#refs}
:::
